§5.5 Matrix Spaces

Vector spaces, are not really spaces of vectors. As long as any objects have a “good” addition and multiplication, ie. satisfying these core properties Definition 5.1.6.

Therefore, the atomic objects at study could very well be matrices.

Example 5.5.1.

The space of real matrices is a vector space. It has dimensions, each basis could be a matrix with the only non-zero element a , each at a distinct position.

Example 5.5.2.

The union of the subspace of symmetric matrices and upper triangular matrices , both having dimension of , is also a subspace, of dimension . And, the space of symmetric plus upper triangular, , is also a vector space (see Problem 5.1.1), with dimension .

Remark.

Interestingly,

Example 5.5.3.

Describe the solution space to the second-order ordinary differential equation

Solution.

The solutions to this ODE form a vector space. In fact, its two basis vectors are and . With tools of linear algebra, one may remark that the solution space is 2 dimensional, with basis vectors and . Therefore, the solutions could be described as .

Remark.

Thus, one insight stemming from this example is that, to solve a linear differential equation, it suffices to find the basis vectors to its solution space. Also, the dimensions of the solution space is equal to the order of the linear differential equations.

Example 5.5.4.

Consider the space of matrices, does the space formed by all rank matrices form a subspace.

Solution.

No. The sum two rank matrices may not be of rank .

Example 5.5.5.

Consider four vectors, , and , show that forms a vector space and find the basis of this vector space.

Solution.

Notice that scalar multiplication preserves the fact that four vectors sum to and addition also preserves that.

Notice that this space is of three dimensions. Also notice that this is the null space of , where

Since .
